justification: Corruption perception is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning retrospective assessment, future expectations, and perceived intractability, so no single item captures it fully. p2, p3, and p34 share a conceptually coherent attitudinal core—all tap the respondent's overall pessimism/optimism about corruption as a societal phenomenon—and their ordinal scales (worse/same/better type) are sufficiently compatible for PCA extraction of a latent severity dimension. p15 is excluded due to near-zero entropy (H=0.46, essentially a constant), and p16/p17 measure institutional location rather than perceived severity, making them construct-misaligned for a perception intensity variable.

Tolerance of corrupt behavior is best captured by attitudinal items that directly ask whether rule-bending is acceptable or constitutes corruption, rather than behavioral or motivational items. p10_1 (tolerating traffic violations), p13_1 (endorsing the 'cheaters prosper' norm), and p9_1 (classifying personal phone use as corruption) all use agree/disagree or yes/no scales measuring normative acceptance of minor infractions, making them scale-compatible and conceptually coherent for a latent tolerance factor. p14, p12, and p19 are excluded because they measure reported behavior or attributed motivations rather than personal normative tolerance, introducing construct contamination.

p23 has the highest Shannon entropy (H=2.57) among all civic enforcement items and is the gold-standard face-valid measure: it asks what the respondent actually did when peers cheated on an exam, directly operationalizing the willingness to enforce norms in a personally relevant social context. The remaining items span highly heterogeneous response formats—hypothetical punishment recommendations for others (p20–p22) versus self-reported behavioral reactions (p24, p11)—mixing normative prescription with behavioral description in ways that make aggregation conceptually muddled. A single high-entropy behavioral item avoids scale incompatibility and provides a clean, actionable bridge variable.

Institutional trust and efficacy is best operationalized by combining the two 0–10 numeric rating scales (p6_1: honesty of priests as a civil-society anchor; p36_1: government effectiveness against corruption), which are directly scale-compatible and together span both societal and governmental trust dimensions. Averaging these two items yields a continuous latent trust-efficacy score that can be binned into ordinal categories for the bridge model. p25_1 and p18 measure knowledge of reporting channels and perceived enforcement location rather than trust intensity, and p35_1 has very low entropy (H=0.97) and taps responsibility attribution rather than trust, so all three are excluded to preserve construct validity.

p32—reasons for staying silent about authority abuse—is the gold-standard measure of reporting barriers because it directly captures the motivational and structural obstacles that suppress reporting, which is the theoretically central mechanism linking SES to under-reporting of corruption. While p33 has higher entropy (H=2.52), it conditions on having reported, making it applicable only to a small sub-sample and introducing severe selection bias in a bridge regression. Aggregating across p27–p33 is inappropriate because the items form a conditional funnel (victimization → reporting → barriers → outcomes) with incompatible scales and drastically different eligible sub-populations at each step.
